slides.headline: Simulating Open Democracy
slides.subhead: Comparing Deliberation Engines in a Shared Argument-Theoretic Environment
slides.notes: Central comparative claim: holding the forum, argument pool, and outcome metrics fixed, cognitive implementation changes the kind of deliberation an ABM produces. The talk reports one controlled test of that claim.

blocks.label: Author
blocks.text: Ignacio Urbina · Stony Brook University · April 2026

slides.headline: Deliberation exchanges reasons; ABMs rarely model that exchange.
slides.subhead: The deliberative ideal demands public reason-giving (Habermas 1996; Dryzek 2010; Mansbridge et al. 2012); real forums show that design levers matter but co-vary (Barabas 2004; Fishkin et al. 2021; Niemeyer et al. 2024).
slides.notes: The manuscript's introduction opens with the normative claim and then steps into the practical problem: in heterogeneous settings like local councils, school boards, or community forums, the coordination cost of reason-giving often exceeds the perceived benefit of participation, leaving policy choices to thin participation, survey aggregation, or non-decision. Niemeyer et al. (2024) make the bundling explicit by comparing forums that vary on group building, complexity, duration, and closure. The methodological gap is that deliberation is too interactive to study as a list of independent treatments, yet empirical forums are too bundled to reveal how each lever works on its own.

items.title: Normative
items.detail: Deliberation = public exchange of reasons, not preference aggregation (Habermas 1996; Mansbridge et al. 2012).

items.title: Empirical
items.detail: Mini-publics confirm facilitation, composition, and closure matter — but bundle them (Fishkin et al. 2021; Niemeyer et al. 2024).

items.title: Classical ABMs
items.detail: Opinion-dynamics models isolate mechanisms but fix cognition in the update rule (DeGroot 1974; Deffuant et al. 2000; Hegselmann & Krause 2002).

items.title: Argument-theoretic ABMs
items.detail: Take reasons as primitive, but rarely vary cognition under a shared protocol (Gabbriellini & Torroni 2014; Shugars 2021).

blocks.label: Research question
blocks.text: Does cognitive implementation change the kind of deliberation an ABM produces, once the forum, argument pool, and outcome metrics are held fixed?

slides.headline: Argument-theoretic state space.
slides.subhead: Considerations \(c = (\ell_c,\, d_c,\, s_c)\) with direction \(d_c \in [-1,1]\) and persuasiveness \(s_c \in [0,1]\); sparse signed weights \(w_{ic} \in [-1,1]\) over a repertoire \(R_i \subseteq \mathcal{C}\) (cf. Dung 1995; Gabbriellini & Torroni 2014).
slides.notes: Opinions are derived from weighted reasons; the model does not permit free-floating opinion motion. Any shift in expressed stance must be traced to changes in weights over considerations. This reason-centered ontology is what allows the paper to evaluate grounded convergence rather than endpoint movement alone.

slides.headline: Voice → Evaluate → Reflect, one voice per agent per round.
slides.subhead: The three hooks are the abstraction boundary: any cognitive engine implementing them plugs into the same state, protocol, and outcome metrics.
slides.notes: Voice: agent selects one held consideration. Evaluate: listeners score its influence given their currently held attackers and supporters. Reflect: weights move toward endorsement or rejection at a rate set by persuasiveness and agent inertia; previously unheld considerations can be adopted when influence clears a threshold. The rule-based engine realizes these hooks via attack-support arithmetic on the graph; the LLM engine realizes them via tool-constrained action calls.

items.title: Voice
items.detail: Select one held consideration to speak; budget = 1 per round.

items.title: Evaluate
items.detail: Score heard consideration's influence given held attackers and supporters.

items.title: Reflect
items.detail: Update weights toward endorsement or rejection; adopt new considerations if influence clears threshold.

slides.headline: A single round, step by step.
slides.subhead: Illustrative, not a real trace. Same three hooks drive both engines; the natural-language surface is what differs.
slides.notes: Walk through this slide by advancing reveals. The scenario: round 3 of 8 in a polarized room, six agents. A Pro-Coherent agent speaks; a Con-Coherent listener has an attacker in their repertoire; an Ambivalent-Mixed listener has neither attacker nor supporter and clears the adoption threshold. Reflect phase: the ambivalent listener adopts the new consideration; the Pro speaker's weight is reinforced; the Con listener's weight stays inside the no-update band. This illustrates three things the rest of the talk depends on: (i) voice is selection from a sparse repertoire, (ii) evaluation is graph-conditional, (iii) reflection can adopt new reasons, not just reweight old ones.

messages.text: A well-designed minimum-wage floor raises standards without closing small firms; the burden is absorbed across payroll, not concentrated on marginal workers.
Influence low: I already hold an attacker ('small-business margins are razor-thin').
Influence high: no attackers or supporters for c₄ in my repertoire yet; persuasive on its face.
Add c₄ to R₆ with weight w₆,c₄ = +0.38.
messages.annotation: \(|R_6|: 5 \to 6\); new consideration flagged `_adopted = true` in the trace.

messages.text: No counter-pressure heard; weight on c₄ ticks up from +0.62 to +0.68.

slides.headline: Seattle 2014 $15/hour Polis conversation.
slides.subhead: Computational Democracy Project open-data release (pol.is/2demo); a documented controversy, not a binding mini-public.
slides.notes: CompDemocracy documents this case as a long-running demonstration conversation rather than an official City of Seattle mini-public, but it is tied to Seattle's real 2014 fight over the $15 minimum wage — phase-in schedule, employer coverage, tips and benefits, enforcement. This gives the model access to publicly available reasons on both sides of a live policy controversy rather than to an abstract survey prompt. The three compositions test whether the engine comparison holds across different room structures. Symmetric rooms have no coherent anchors — all agents hold mixed or ambivalent consideration sets. Polarized rooms plant tight ideological anchors at both poles. Three-cluster rooms add a true center: two agents who are ambivalent on both policy and coherence, flanked by one coherent and one mixed agent on each side.

items.label: Participants
items.detail: 108 pass inclusion filter (≥ 3 votes on selected considerations)

items.label: Statements
items.detail: moderated statements retained

items.label: Agents per room
items.detail: drawn from the 3×3 policy–coherence grid

items.label: Symmetric
items.detail: 2 Pro/Mixed · 1 Pro/Ambiv · 2 Con/Mixed · 1 Con/Ambiv

items.label: Polarized
items.detail: 2 Pro/Coherent · 1 Pro/Mixed · 2 Con/Coherent · 1 Con/Mixed

items.label: Three-cluster
items.detail: 1 Pro/Coh · 1 Pro/Mix · 2 Ambiv/Ambiv · 1 Con/Mix · 1 Con/Coh

slides.headline: Ten considerations, drawn from 30 moderated statements via semantic clustering.
slides.subhead: Each consideration \(c = (\ell_c,\, d_c,\, s_c)\): a statement label, a direction (pro/con), and a persuasiveness score derived from the cross-over endorsement rate (opposing-side agreement via IRT), normalized to \([0.3,\, 0.9]\).
slides.notes: These are verbatim Polis statements written by participants during Seattle's 2014 $15/hour fight — not researcher-authored prompts. Selection procedure: the 29 moderated statements are embedded with e5-large-v2, then clustered into 10 topical groups via hierarchical agglomerative clustering (cosine distance, average linkage). Within each cluster, we pick the statement with the highest separation power — defined as max over other clusters C' of mean |Q(i,j)| for j in C'. This ensures the representative is both topically representative and maximally opinion-active across clusters. Ties broken by number of directional votes. Persuasiveness is the cross-over endorsement rate: the share of opposing-side participants (by IRT theta) who agreed, rescaled so that the weakest item still exerts some pull (0.3) and the strongest does not dominate (0.9). The audience should notice two things: (i) the pro side has stronger cross-camp reach on average, and (ii) the weakest argument in the pool (C₃, inflation-penalizes-the-rich) is also the most easily attacked — a correlation the model inherits from the data, not from a design choice.

items.label: C₅ · Pro · s = 0.73
items.value: "Something needs to be done to address income inequality and this is a good if imperfect start."
items.detail: Cross-over rate 40%; high reach into opposing camp

items.label: C₁ · Pro · s = 0.82
items.value: "Businesses shouldn't be able to pay workers anything below a livable wage. Why should we subsidize businesses that depend on inadequate wages?"
items.detail: Cross-over rate 47%; attacks C₃ and C₆

items.label: C₇ · Con · s = 0.77
items.value: "It's just going to speed up the adoption of robotics in industries with unskilled or low-skilled workers."
items.detail: Cross-over rate 43%; automation-threat framing

items.label: C₃ · Con · s = 0.30
items.value: "$15 min wage will lead to inflation. Higher income population is penalized for this reason and therefore I don't think it is fair."
items.detail: Cross-over rate 3%; weakest consideration in the pool

slides.headline: Attack and support edges emerge from observed voting, not researcher judgment.
slides.subhead: Yule's \(Q\) on each statement pair: \(Q = (n_{\text{concordant}} - n_{\text{discordant}}) / (n_{\text{concordant}} + n_{\text{discordant}})\). Negative \(Q\) → attack; positive \(Q\) → support. Threshold \(|Q| \geq 0.2\), minimum 10 co-voters.
slides.notes: The graph has 15 attack pairs and 19 support pairs across 10 nodes. Attack edges fall almost exclusively between the pro block (C₁, C₂, C₄, C₅, C₈) and the con block (C₃, C₆, C₇, C₉, C₁₀); support edges fall within each block. The strongest attack (Q = −0.69) links C₁ (livable-wage mandate) to C₁₀ (reactionary-liberal critique); the weakest retained support (Q = +0.23) links C₉ (self-service) to C₁₀ (systemic-critique). This bipartite-like structure is an empirical result — the Polis electorate sorted itself into two co-voting camps, and the model inherits that polarization through the graph rather than imposing it.

items.label: Attack example: C₁ ↔ C₁₀
items.detail: "Livable wage" vs. "Reactionary liberal trash" — voters who endorsed one opposed the other

items.label: Support example: C₅ ↔ C₂
items.detail: "Good start on inequality" & "Workers must keep fighting inflation" — co-endorsed

nodes.label: Businesses, small or large, shouldn't be able to pay workers anything below a livable wage. Why should we subsidize businesses that depend on inadequate wages?
Given inflation, unless workers fight they are continually being pushed down. We'll need to revisit these laws again and again.
75% of minimum-wage workers are 20 or older. It's the right thing to do to bring higher incomes to this group.
Something needs to be done to address income inequality and this is a good if imperfect start.
One step towards a world where a basic minimum income will be the way developed nations must function.
$15 min wage will lead to inflation. Higher income population is penalized and therefore I don't think it is fair.
Higher wages makes it super hard for small businesses to survive. Mom and pop coffee shops will disappear.
It's just going to speed up the adoption of robotics in industries with unskilled or low-skilled workers.
New businesses will run counter operations where guests grab their own silverware and bus their own tables.
Reactionary liberal trash. Doesn't solve the problem. It's a systemic problem and this solution is like a band aid on a broken leg.

blocks.label: Edge strength
blocks.text: \(\text{strength} = 0.2 + 0.6 \times \min(|Q|,\, 1)\); maps co-vote intensity to \([0.2,\, 0.8]\). Persuasiveness \(s_c\) is the cross-over endorsement rate (opposing-side agreement); edge strength is the co-vote correlation — they measure different things.

slides.headline: One vote matrix grounds pool, graph, and initialization.
slides.subhead: Sparse repertoires via Ising-based profile reconstruction. Sparse init mean \(|R_i^{(0)}| \approx 5.4\) of \(10\); new considerations enter later through Reflect.
slides.notes: Three distinct uses of the same Polis export: (1) moderated statements supply the argument pool (previous two slides); (2) pairwise co-voting (Yule's Q) identifies attack/support structure, and cross-over endorsement rates (opposing-side agreement via IRT theta) set item persuasiveness; (3) an Ising-based reconstruction of belief profiles seeds agent initialization. The Ising model treats the Q matrix as coupling strengths and imputes unobserved votes via mean-field inference — a participant who agreed with three pro statements and disagreed with one con statement gets predicted soft votes on the remaining six. Coherence is the z-scored negative energy of the full profile: high-coherence agents hold tightly aligned consideration sets, low-coherence agents hold contradictory ones. Sparsity matters because the model does not begin from full information — it begins from unequal, empirically grounded informational endowments.

cells: reasons available to voice and adopt
intrinsic persuasive reach into opposing camp
sparse starting repertoires and coherence scores

slides.headline: Pooled finite-difference indexed model, clustered by run.
slides.subhead: 964 runs (600 rule-based, 364 LLM) over 8 rounds, \(n=6\) agents each; 6,748 adjacent run-round observations. Arms interacted with composition; outcomes indexed as \(\text{index}^{(k)}_{it} = 100 + 100\bigl(y^{(k)}_{it} - y^{(k)}_{i,t_0}\bigr)\).
slides.notes: Identical town-hall protocol, argument pool, round count, and outcome metrics across arms. The empirical rule-based engine is grounded through the co-vote structure; the LLM engine is tool-constrained (no gold demonstrations, no task-specific fine-tuning). Arm sample sizes are uneven by design stage (600 vs. 364), so comparisons are reported as directional slope contrasts rather than point-estimate decompositions. Clustered standard errors at the run level (Cameron & Miller 2015). Index-point interpretation: an index-point change of +5 means the outcome grew 5 percentage points of its baseline distance over the deliberation period. All outcomes are normalized to a common 100-point baseline so cross-outcome comparisons use comparable units.

slides.headline: LLM engine: positive slope gaps on reason-level outcomes.
slides.subhead: Deliberative Reasoning Index (Niemeyer et al. 2024; Veri & Niemeyer 2025) and meta-consensus on salience (Curato et al. 2017). Triplets: polarized / symmetric / three-cluster.
slides.notes: DRI asks whether opinion similarity is grounded in shared reason structure — it is the correlation between pairwise weight-vector cosine similarity and opinion proximity. Meta-consensus tracks convergence on what matters rather than what to do — measured as agreement on consideration salience (absolute weight magnitude) across agents. The LLM signature is largest on three-cluster rooms for DRI and uniformly positive and statistically significant on meta-consensus. Two supporting outcomes are held off-slide: plurality moves downward in the LLM arm (the rule-based arm shows mixed or flat movement); better-argument-wins also favors the LLM arm but is not part of the core epistemic-alignment claim. An index-point change of +5 means the outcome grew 5 percentage points of its baseline distance over the deliberation period.

blocks.label: Measures
blocks.text: DRI: correlation between pairwise opinion similarity and shared reason structure — do agents who agree also agree on why? Meta-consensus: convergence on what matters, not what to do.

items.label: DRI gap (LLM − rule)
items.detail: polarized / symmetric / three-cluster; three-cluster largest (p = .006)

items.label: Meta-consensus gap
items.detail: polarized / symmetric / three-cluster; significant in all three

slides.headline: Rule-based engine: negative slope gaps on circulation outcomes.
slides.subhead: Argument diversity and cross-camp uptake (cf. Stasser & Vaughan 1996; Mercier & Landemore 2012). Triplets: polarized / symmetric / three-cluster.
slides.notes: Argument diversity rises under both engines but much more under the rule-based arm; the gap is roughly −21 index points in every composition (all p < .001). Cross-camp uptake leans rule-based in symmetric (−5.19, p = .001), is imprecise in three-cluster (−2.23, p = .148), and near zero in polarized (−0.10, p = .934). Plurality (held off-slide): the LLM arm compresses more; the rule-based arm is mixed or flat. The pattern is argumentative activation and circulation without the same reason-level consolidation observed in the LLM arm.

items.label: Argument diversity gap (LLM − rule)
items.detail: polarized / symmetric / three-cluster; rule-based advantage in every composition; all p < .001

items.label: Cross-camp uptake gap
items.detail: polarized / symmetric / three-cluster; clearest in symmetric (p = .001)

slides.headline: Different cognitive regimes, identical forums.
slides.subhead: Epistemic integration and argumentative activation can come apart (Mansbridge et al. 2012; Landemore 2020).
slides.notes: The two engines instantiate different models of successful deliberation, not different parameterizations of one mechanism. This is a directional comparative exercise — one issue domain, six-agent rooms, uneven arms — not a causal decomposition. The contribution is framework-level: a shared architecture in which institutional rules and cognitive implementation can be separately varied. For Landemore's (2020) open-democracy agenda, this matters because randomly-selected bodies need ways to inspect whether visible agreement is grounded in shared justificatory structure.

items: LLM engine → stronger reason-level alignment (DRI, meta-consensus).
Rule-based engine → stronger argumentative circulation (diversity, uptake).
Composition moderates magnitude; the claim is directional, not uniform.

blocks.label: Scope
blocks.text: Bounded comparative exercise — one issue, six-agent rooms, 600 vs. 364 runs. Engine choice is a design result, not a technical detail.
